metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the unit of `COMPACTION_HORIZON` in the plan (nanoseconds to match HLC ticks, i.e. default `1_800_000_000_000` ns = 1800 s), and specify the conversion in the ADR so the horizon comparison `now_hlc - event_hlc >= COMPACTION_HORIZON` is dimensionally correct.
findings.finding: COMPACTION_HORIZON unit is underspecified relative to HLC timestamps: HLC ticks are nanoseconds (time.time_ns()), so comparing a raw HLC delta against the integer 1800 (documented as '1800s = 30 min') is trivially true and makes the guard a no-op — all events pass the horizon and nothing is protected. The plan gives the default as '1800s' without specifying whether the constant is in seconds, milliseconds, or nanoseconds, or that the implementer must scale it.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3 / COMPACTION_HORIZON), AC: 'COMPACTION_HORIZON constant defined + documented (default 1800s, configurable)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define `COMPACTION_HORIZON` in nanoseconds (e.g. `COMPACTION_HORIZON = 1_800_000_000_000`) and document it as '1800 s expressed in HLC nanosecond ticks, matching `hlc.physical_now()` units'. Update the AC and ADR to state the unit explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete implementation of the `include_retired` rebuild path: either add a `include_retired: bool = False` kwarg to `reduce_ticket` (and thread it through `prepare_event_files` / `_cache.py`), or define a separate internal function — and name it explicitly in the plan so A3 can depend on a stable interface.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` calling convention as the rebuild mechanism, but no such parameter or overload exists anywhere in the codebase — `reduce_ticket` in `_api.py` accepts only `ticket_dir_path`. This interface must be designed and added as new work, yet the plan describes it as a settled API path without specifying how it should be implemented (new kwarg on `reduce_ticket`, a separate `reduce_ticket_with_retired()` function, or a lower-level call bypassing `_cache.py`'s glob).
findings.location: REBUILD section: 'a `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` path; reduce_ticket today EXCLUDES `.retired`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the rebuild dispatch site to scope — either `_commands/fsck.py` (extending `_check_snapshot` to call rebuild) or a new `_commands/fsck_rebuild.py` — and confirm it is wired to the existing `ORPHAN_EVENT` detection logic.
findings.finding: The rebuild trigger/dispatch site is not named in scope: the plan says 'fsck detects a merged-in pre-snapshot orphan … and rebuilds that ticket's snapshot' but `fsck.py` is absent from the scope list. The existing `_check_snapshot` in `fsck.py` already emits `ORPHAN_EVENT` lines — the plan must extend `fsck.py` (or name an alternative dispatch file) to trigger the rebuild, but no such file is listed.
findings.location: Scope section / REBUILD bullet / src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:184-236

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC or Scope entry stating that `reduce_ticket` must gain an `include_retired=True` parameter (threading through `prepare_event_files`/`_cache.py` glob), and verify the signature before calling it in the rebuild path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` as the rebuild path for the orphan-repair mechanism, but the current `reduce_ticket` signature accepts only `(ticket_dir_path: str | os.PathLike[str])` with no keyword parameters whatsoever. The `include_retired=True` overload does not exist and is asserted as if it already does.
findings.location: REBUILD (Option 1) section: 'a `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` path; reduce_ticket today EXCLUDES `.retired`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '`reducer/_api.py` and `reducer/_cache.py` — add `include_retired: bool = False` kwarg to `reduce_ticket` and thread it through `prepare_event_files` so the rebuild path can pass `include_retired=True` to include `*.retired` files in the replay glob.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Unit test: `reduce_ticket(ticket_dir, include_retired=True)` sees `.retired` source events; `reduce_ticket(ticket_dir)` (default) does not.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact new `reduce_ticket` signature extension (e.g. `include_retired: bool = False` keyword arg, or a separate `reduce_ticket_with_retired()` function) in the plan, specifying which module owns it and what `prepare_event_files` change is required to enumerate `.retired` files.
findings.finding: `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` is referenced as the rebuild mechanism but this parameter does not exist anywhere in the codebase — `reduce_ticket` in `reducer/_api.py` accepts only `ticket_dir_path: str | os.PathLike[str]` and has no `include_retired` parameter or overload.
findings.location: REBUILD (Option 1) section: 'a reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True) path; reduce_ticket today EXCLUDES .retired'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'reducer/_api.py: add `include_retired: bool = False` kwarg to `reduce_ticket`; when True, `prepare_event_files` also globs `*.json.retired` (or `*.retired`) and inserts them into the sorted event list.' Confirm the exact file suffix matches A1's `RETIRED_SUFFIX`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reducer/_cache.py` to the scope: `prepare_event_files` (or a new `include_retired` variant) must explicitly enumerate `*.retired` files when the rebuild path is invoked.
findings.finding: `_cache.py` (`prepare_event_files`) is an unenumerated edit target: its two file-listing paths (`os.listdir` filter on line 97 and `glob.glob('*.json')` on line 102) both exclude `*.retired` files by construction — neither will surface them for the `include_retired=True` rebuild path the plan introduces in `_api.py`. Without updating `_cache.py`, the new `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` parameter is a no-op.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py:97,102
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `include_retired: bool = False` parameter to `prepare_event_files` in `_cache.py`; when True, include files matching `*.retired` in both the `event_filenames` list and the `glob.glob` sort pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact primitive used to read 'now' for the horizon comparison (e.g. `physical_now()` from `hlc.py`) and state explicitly that `COMPACTION_HORIZON` is stored/compared in nanoseconds (i.e. `1_800_000_000_000` ns = 30 min) or that the difference is divided by `1e9` before comparison.
findings.finding: The horizon comparison rule `now_hlc - event_hlc >= COMPACTION_HORIZON` uses an undefined `now_hlc` symbol. The HLC module (`_store/hlc.py`) exposes only `next_tick(tracker, ticket_id)` (a write-advancing function) and `physical_now()` — neither is a read-only 'current HLC time' query. The comparison's unit (seconds vs nanoseconds) is also unspecified despite HLC timestamps being nanosecond integers.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3): 'compaction folds an event only if now_hlc - event_hlc >= COMPACTION_HORIZON'
findings.suggested_fix: In Concrete Rules: 'Horizon guard: `physical_now() - event_hlc >= COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` where `COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS = COMPACTION_HORIZON * 1_000_000_000`. Both `physical_now()` and the event timestamp prefix are nanosecond integers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism by which compact.py determines that a successful fetch occurred (e.g., a stamp file written by the fetch step at a known path, or an exit-code passed as a CLI argument), and add it to the Scope/Concrete Rules.
findings.finding: The plan states compact.py guards folding on 'the clone completed >=1 successful git fetch origin/tickets this pass', but provides no mechanism for compact.py to detect or record that a successful fetch occurred — this is a runtime tracking problem with no described solution.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3) — conservative horizon fold guard

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for detecting a 'successful git fetch' in compact.py: either (a) have `_sync_before_compact` return a bool success flag (and document the impact on its other callers), or (b) check the throttle-marker timestamp as a proxy for 'a fetch ran this session', rather than leaving the gate mechanically unspecified.
findings.finding: The 'clone completed >=1 successful git fetch origin/tickets this pass' gate in compact.py has no mechanical specification. The existing `ensure_fresh()` / `_sync_before_compact()` return `None` unconditionally (all errors swallowed), so there is no existing success signal to check — the plan implicitly requires either modifying ensure_fresh or adding a new tracking layer, but names neither.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3): 'AND the clone completed >=1 successful `git fetch origin/tickets` this pass'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name the file(s) where fetch-success tracking state is implemented/persisted (e.g., extending the HLC state or throttle marker in `_store/sync.py` or `_engine_support/reads.py`), or document that the fetch-gate condition is dropped/simplified in the final design.
findings.finding: The 'clone completed >=1 successful `git fetch origin/tickets` this pass' gate in the compaction horizon rule names a mechanism that does not exist in the codebase: `ensure_fresh` in `reads.py` (the sole fetch abstraction) has no success counter, no persistent state of fetch outcomes, and no per-pass tracking. No file is named in scope for implementing this.
findings.location: Concrete rules (SETTLED / Option 3) / src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:186-251

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add the `ensure_fresh`/`reconverge` scope to this plan (return a fetch-success bool) so `compact.py` can gate on it, or (b) restate the precondition in terms of something already observable (e.g. 'remote ref exists and is newer than the local HLC high-water mark') to avoid the unspecified API change.
findings.finding: The 'successful `git fetch origin/tickets` this pass' precondition for the horizon guard has no surfaced signal in the existing `ensure_fresh`/`reconverge` API. Both functions swallow all errors and return `None` (BLE001 broad-catch). There is no way for `compact.py` to know whether the fetch that ran inside `_sync_before_compact` succeeded or was skipped/throttled/failed — the plan requires this as a gate condition but doesn't call out that `ensure_fresh` or `reconverge` must be extended to return a fetch-success flag, which is an out-of-scope API change.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3), Scope section ('`_commands/compact.py` (conservative-horizon fold guard)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `compact.py` will observe fetch success (e.g., `_sync_before_compact` returns a bool, or a new `fetch_succeeded` flag is threaded through), since `reconverge`/`ensure_fresh` currently return `None` and swallow all errors.
findings.finding: The compaction horizon gate requires knowing whether 'the clone completed >=1 successful `git fetch origin/tickets` this pass', but the existing `ensure_fresh` / `reconverge` path in `rebar._store.sync` is entirely best-effort and always returns `None`, swallowing all errors silently. There is no API to observe fetch success, making the gate condition unimplementable as described without a new observable signal.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3): 'AND the clone completed >=1 successful `git fetch origin/tickets` this pass'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `include_retired=True` path in `reduce_ticket` invalidates or bypasses `.cache.json` (or extends `dir_hash` to include `.retired` file metadata), so a cache hit cannot suppress a rebuild that depends on `.retired` files.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` as a new call signature, but the existing `reduce_ticket` function in `reducer/_api.py` has no `include_retired` parameter and the current `prepare_event_files` / `_cache.py` glob excludes `.retired` files. The plan does not specify how the existing `.cache.json` invalidation handles the newly-visible `.retired` files — a cache hit computed without `.retired` files could be served for a ticket that now needs a rebuild including them, silently suppressing the orphan fix.
findings.location: REBUILD (Option 1) section — 'reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True) path; reduce_ticket today EXCLUDES .retired'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the fetch-completion gate: compaction MUST NOT fold events on a pass where no successful `git fetch origin/tickets` occurred (regardless of event age), with a unit/integration test covering this branch.
findings.finding: The 'fetch-completion gate' condition (clone must have completed >=1 successful git fetch) is described in Concrete Rules as a required condition for compaction folding, but no AC covers it — it is described work with no corresponding criterion.
findings.location: Concrete Rules (Option 3 / conservative horizon) vs. Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Unit test: an event older than COMPACTION_HORIZON is NOT folded when the clone has zero successful fetches recorded for the current pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `include_retired=True` should be an internal helper (e.g. `_reduce_ticket_with_retired()` in the rebuild module) rather than a new parameter on the public `reduce_ticket` API, to avoid polluting 40+ existing call-sites with a parameter that currently has <3 consumers.
findings.finding: The `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` parameter is proposed on a 40+ call-site public API, but only 2 callers are named (the rebuild path in this plan + A3). This falls short of Rule-of-Three and risks polluting a heavily-used public signature for a specialized internal use-case.
findings.location: Scope section / REBUILD bullet: '`reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` path; reduce_ticket today EXCLUDES `.retired`'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a private `_rebuild_reduce(ticket_dir, *, include_retired=True)` function local to the fsck/rebuild module that handles the `.retired` glob internally, reusing `replay_events` directly, rather than adding a new optional parameter to the public `reduce_ticket` signature.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete deletion trigger for `.bak` files (which process, what signal constitutes 'clean replay round-trip'), and specify an orphan-GC path for `.bak` files left behind by a crashed rebuild.
findings.finding: The `.bak` file retirement path is underspecified: the plan states the old snapshot is moved to `.bak` and 'deleted only after a clean replay round-trip,' but it does not define (a) which process is responsible for the deletion, (b) what constitutes a 'clean replay round-trip' as a concrete detectable signal, or (c) how `.bak` files orphaned by a crash between the snapshot rename and the deletion are identified and cleaned up. Without a defined retirement trigger and orphan-GC path, `.bak` files accumulate indefinitely.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD) — '`.bak`, deleted only after a clean replay round-trip'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: after a successful fsck round that finds zero orphans for a ticket, any `.bak` for that ticket is eligible for deletion; fsck itself performs the deletion and logs it. On startup, fsck flags stale `.bak` files (older than 2× COMPACTION_HORIZON) for operator review or auto-removal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (specifically `CompactConfig` and the `_SECTIONS['compact']` coercion table) to the scope, to wire `compaction_horizon` as a configurable integer field.
findings.finding: `_config_schema.py` (`CompactConfig`) is an unenumerated edit target: the plan requires `COMPACTION_HORIZON` to be 'configurable' (AC: 'default 1800s, configurable'), but `CompactConfig` currently has only `threshold: int = 10` and `_config_schema.py` is not in the plan's scope list.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/_config_schema.py:313-315 / AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add `compaction_horizon: int = 1800` to `CompactConfig` and add `'compaction_horizon': lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=1)` to `_SECTIONS['compact']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs for: (a) the `.bak` file is retained until a clean replay round-trip succeeds (and is then deleted); and (b) a rebuild counter is exposed and incremented per detected orphan/rebuild.
findings.finding: The `.bak` deletion step ('deleted only after a clean replay round-trip') and the rebuild counter ('expose a rebuild counter') are described deliverables with no covering ACs.
findings.location: REBUILD section vs. Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the proving command for AC6: name the output artifact ('compiled state'), e.g. `python -m reducer compile --out /tmp/run1.json && python -m reducer compile --out /tmp/run2.json && diff <(sha256sum /tmp/run1.json) <(sha256sum /tmp/run2.json)`, and add a unit test that shuffles directory listing and asserts hash equality.
findings.finding: AC6 ('Determinism proof: replay twice on the same file set (incl. shuffled directory-listing order) -> byte-identical compiled state') has no proving command, and 'compiled state' (the artifact to compare) is undefined — no described step reaches this end-state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify the concrete change to `reducer/_sort.py` (e.g., extend `event_sort_key` to handle `.retired` suffixes so retired events sort into their original position) or remove it from scope if no change is needed.
findings.finding: `reducer/_sort.py` is listed in scope but no concrete change to it is described anywhere in the plan — no new sort key, no new symbol, no behavioral modification is articulated. If no change is actually needed, listing it creates confusion about edit intent; if a change is needed (e.g., to sort `.retired` files into the event sequence), the change is unspecified.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/reducer/_sort.py
findings.suggested_fix: If `.retired` files must sort with their originals during rebuild replay, note that `event_sort_key` should strip the `.retired` suffix before computing the sort key so `1234-uuid-STATUS.json.retired` sorts identically to `1234-uuid-STATUS.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rebuild counter's exact placement and exposure mechanism (e.g. 'a `rebuild_count` integer in the fsck JSON output under `fixed`, or a structured `logger.warning` with a `rebuild_count=N` field, or a module-level metric in `_commands/fsck_recover.py`') and add an AC that verifies it is observable.
findings.finding: The plan says 'expose a rebuild counter' but specifies no placement (module, variable name, scope, or exposure mechanism — e.g. logger metric, in-memory counter, structured log field, or fsck output field).
findings.location: REBUILD (Option 1): 'Log (WARN) each detected orphan + each rebuild; expose a rebuild counter.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile 'pre-snapshot orphan' and 'pre-horizon orphan' terminology: explicitly state whether these are the same condition (an event that merged in after compaction and sorts positionally before the snapshot) and use one term consistently throughout the plan and ACs.
findings.finding: Terminology drift between 'pre-snapshot orphan' (positional concept — event sorts before the SNAPSHOT record) and 'pre-horizon orphan' (temporal concept — event is younger than COMPACTION_HORIZON) risks implementer confusion about WHICH check triggers rebuild.
findings.location: Problem statement / REBUILD section / AC line 4 ('pre-horizon orphan')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pre-horizon orphan' in AC 4 with 'pre-snapshot orphan (a KNOWN-type .json event sorting before the SNAPSHOT whose uuid is absent from source_event_uuids)' to match the REBUILD section language.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the explicit back-out procedure: if the post-rebuild replay round-trip fails, the system must automatically restore the snapshot from `.bak` (or halt and alert), and this must be documented in the plan and covered by a test or AC.
findings.finding: The `.bak` rollback mechanism is acknowledged but the back-out procedure on failure is unspecified: the plan does not state what happens if the 'clean replay round-trip' fails — whether `.bak` is automatically restored, an alert is raised, or the system is left in a degraded state.
findings.location: REBUILD section — atomic rename / .bak lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: Add to REBUILD section: 'If the clean replay round-trip fails, restore snapshot from .bak and log ERROR; do not delete .bak until restoration is confirmed. Add a test asserting this rollback path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement step (e.g. sample observed `git fetch` round-trip latency from live clone logs) to empirically derive or bound the 1800s default before it is committed as the shipped constant.
findings.finding: The `COMPACTION_HORIZON` default of 1800s (30 min) is asserted as '>= 2x worst-case clone sync latency' by reasoning alone, with no measurement plan or data point to derive or validate this value before it ships to production.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3) / AC: 'COMPACTION_HORIZON constant defined + documented (default 1800s, configurable)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR, record the measurement methodology: e.g., parse existing sync-log timestamps to compute max observed inter-clone latency, then set COMPACTION_HORIZON = max_observed * safety_factor (>= 2). Optionally add an integration-test fixture that asserts the horizon constant is >= the measured p99 latency, so regressions are caught if the network environment changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-rebuild-failure rollback behavior: if the verification round-trip fails (or the process crashes after rename), define whether the store automatically rolls back (promotes .bak to the canonical snapshot name), and assert the schema state after rollback — including that .bak files are ignored by `reduce_ticket`/`fsck` during normal operation.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'old snapshot -> .bak, deleted only after a clean replay round-trip' but does not assert on the schema state after a rebuild failure or rollback: if the rebuilt snapshot is written and the verification round-trip then fails, neither the plan nor the spec says whether the store is rolled back to .bak (restoring the original snapshot) or left with the new (potentially incorrect) snapshot in place alongside an orphaned .bak. A crash or error between atomic rename and verification leaves the store in an ambiguous intermediate state with no defined recovery path.
findings.location: REBUILD (Option 1) section — 'Rebuild writes <snap>.tmp + atomic rename; old snapshot -> .bak, deleted only after a clean replay round-trip'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the REBUILD spec: 'If the post-rebuild round-trip verification fails, the rebuilt snapshot is replaced by .bak (atomic rename back); .bak is deleted on success only. A surviving .bak at fsck time signals an interrupted rebuild — fsck should log a WARN and re-trigger the rebuild. reduce_ticket must exclude *.bak from its event file glob.' This makes the rollback state deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'quiesced' concretely in the Concrete Rules section (e.g., 'no ticket-writer process running, verified by checking a named lock file or empty queue sentinel') so the executor has an unambiguous implementation target.
findings.finding: The concurrency condition 'rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check event log after rebuild' leaves 'quiesced' undefined — an executor cannot implement this without deciding what quiescence means (e.g., no in-flight writers, a file lock, a process check) and how to detect it at runtime.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD) — concurrency sub-bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Quiesced = no concurrent ticket-writer process active; verified by holding/checking a `.rebuild_lock` advisory file or asserting the write-queue depth is zero before rebuilding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sequencing sub-AC to AC5: the regression test must be committed (and CI run) on a branch WITHOUT the fix first to capture the RED result, then the fix is merged; or a `pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)` guard is used as the before-state baseline, lifted when the fix lands.
findings.finding: AC5 ('Two-clone sub-horizon-append data-loss regression test: RED before, GREEN after') requires proving the test was RED against old code, but no sequencing AC, baseline-capture step, or mechanism (e.g., a git stash run or a dedicated fixture flag) is described for producing the RED evidence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Mark the regression test with `@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True, reason='RC2b not yet applied')` in the initial commit, then remove the mark in the fix commit — CI proves RED→GREEN automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the quiescence detection mechanism (e.g., advisory lock on the ticket's snapshot file, or a no-new-events window check) and add a maximum redo iteration count (with WARN log on exhaustion) to the rebuild concurrency spec.
findings.finding: The quiescence detection mechanism for rebuild is unspecified, and the re-check-and-redo loop has no stated termination bound. The plan says 'rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed,' but it never defines how quiescence is detected (a flag, a lock, a no-new-events window?) and does not cap the redo iterations, leaving the loop without an exit condition under sustained event ingestion.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD) — 'Concurrency: rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a per-ticket advisory lock (flock on the snapshot file) held for the duration of the rebuild so concurrent fsck instances serialize rather than race. Cap the redo loop at a small fixed count (e.g., 3) and emit a WARN+skip if exceeded, leaving the ticket for the next fsck cycle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the plan (or ADR) for why the positional skip is retained alongside the rebuild path rather than replaced by pure UUID-set membership gating (e.g. performance on large logs, or replay correctness for deleted/compacted events).
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing rebuild-on-stray (Option 1) over the simpler alternative of removing the positional skip in the replay loop and relying solely on `source_event_uuids` membership to decide which events the SNAPSHOT already covers. The positional skip (`if idx < start_idx: continue` in `replay_events`) is the direct proximate cause of the silent drop; eliminating it and relying purely on UUID-set membership would fix the named bug with less new code and no rebuild machinery. The plan states 'Decision (ACCEPTED): Option 1 + Option 3' without explaining why the positional skip is kept.
findings.location: Decision (ACCEPTED) section; 'REBUILD (Option 1)' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan the exact module path, value, and type signature for `RETIRED_SUFFIX` (e.g. `rebar._store.event_append.RETIRED_SUFFIX: str = '.retired'`) and `is_active_event` (e.g. `(filename: str) -> bool`) that A1 must provide, so RC2b's rebuild path and compaction guard have a concrete interface contract to code against.
findings.finding: `RETIRED_SUFFIX` and `is_active_event` are referenced as already-delivered by dependency A1 ('tricolour-head-ratfish delivers the `.retired` rename + `RETIRED_SUFFIX`/`is_active_event`'), but neither exists anywhere in the codebase today, making them undefined interfaces this plan's implementation depends on without specifying their contract.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'A1 = tricolour-head-ratfish delivers the .retired rename + RETIRED_SUFFIX/is_active_event (depends_on, wired)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
